literal: own-mark; a particular marked only by its own distinctive character
invariant_definition: *Svalakṣaṇa* is the Buddhist pramāṇa term for the momentary particular that is directly given in perception and is real in the strict sense because it alone has determinate causal efficacy. The term therefore does not just mean "particular." It names the unit recognized by a whole epistemological and ontological package: *pratyakṣa* is free of conceptual construction; *anumāna* and language operate on constructed generalities; and only the causally efficacious particular counts as *paramārtha-sat*. This is why the term stands next to *sāmānya-lakṣaṇa*, *apoha*, *kṣaṇika-vāda*, and *artha-kriyā*. In practice the distinction disciplines Buddhist analysis of cognition: what is strictly seen is not yet the classificatory world of "cow," "pot," or "person." That later world is workable, but it is concept-laden. Nyāya rejects the distinction because it takes determinate perception of qualified objects to be basic rather than derivative.

primary_loci: Śaṅkara anti-Buddhist pramāṇa loci [NOT YET RETRIEVED]
Vāchaspati Miśra, Bhāmatī pratyakṣa discussion [NOT YET RETRIEVED]
Rāmānuja, Śrī-Bhāṣya anti-Buddhist loci [NOT YET RETRIEVED]
Madhva / Jayatīrtha anti-Buddhist pramāṇa loci [NOT YET RETRIEVED]

per_school.not_applicable_reason: The retrieved Sāṅkhya occurrences use `svālakṣaṇya` for the own proper function of buddhi, ahaṃkāra, and manas rather than for the Buddhist epistemological category of the momentary perceptual particular.
The assigned Yoga sources do not use `svalakṣaṇa` as a technical category, and no school-specific treatment was retrievable in this corpus pass.

per_school.definition: Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika rejects the thesis that only a bare `svalakṣaṇa` is real or perceptible; Vāchaspati says the alleged contradiction would arise only if the object were merely `svalakṣaṇa`, whereas the real object is stable and possessed of `jāti` and related qualifiers, hence fit both for causal efficacy and for linguistic uptake. [cite://vacaspati/nyaya-varttika-tatparya-tika/1.1.4] Perception therefore does not terminate in an ineffable momentary particular but in a qualified real object. [cite://vacaspati/nyaya-varttika-tatparya-tika/1.1.4]

Bhāṭṭa Mīmāṃsā treats the Buddhist restriction of perception to `svalakṣaṇa` as misplaced: Sucaritamiśra says that while Buddhists specify `svalakṣaṇaviṣayatva`, Mīmāṃsā perception has the connected present object and excludes neither universal nor particular. [cite://sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika-tika/84] He also refuses the claim that perception is not publicly articulable because its object would be an ineffable `svalakṣaṇa`; verbal contradiction and correction can still take the perceived object as their topic. [cite://sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika-tika/pararthanumana-discussion]

For Mādhyamaka, `svalakṣaṇa` is not admitted as a finally established own-being; Candrakīrti argues that if a `svalakṣaṇa` were dependently established, emptiness would become the cause of the destruction of real entities, which is impossible, and therefore no such `bhāva` is established. [cite://candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.34] He accordingly defines `svalakṣaṇa-śūnyatā` as the absence of those very defining marks from all dharmas. [cite://candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.201]

For epistemological Yogācāra, perception has `svalakṣaṇa` as its object, namely that entity whose presence and absence differentiates cognitive appearance, and that alone is `paramārthasat` because a thing is marked by causal efficacy. [cite://dharmakirti/nyaya-bindu/1.12-17] The `sāmānyalakṣaṇa` belongs to inference, so `svalakṣaṇa` is grasped only in avikalpa perception and not through vikalpa. [cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.75]

Pratyabhijñā does not treat `svalakṣaṇa` as an inaccessible atom outside reflective awareness; Abhinavagupta explains that a single manifested object can be analyzed into several appearances, yet the synthesizing `vimarśa` yields one `svalakṣaṇa`, with its articulation varying according to interest, practical need, and learned usage. [cite://abhinavagupta/ishvara-pratyabhijna-vimarshini/2.3.3-commentary] He further says that practical activity bears on the desired `svalakṣaṇa` only through the coordinated aggregate of perceptions of place, time, and the like, so the particular is already embedded in a larger `pramāṇa`-synthesis. [cite://abhinavagupta/ishvara-pratyabhijna-vimarshini/2.3.9-commentary]

primary_loci: Umāsvāti, Tattvārtha-Sūtra [searched; no direct `svalakṣaṇa` locus retrieved]

translator_note: "Particular" is not wrong, but it is too weak by itself. The term means a momentary, causally efficacious particular given in non-conceptual perception. English readers who hear merely "individual thing" will miss the whole Buddhist apparatus around perception, inference, and reality-status. "Bare particular" helps, but it still needs explanation because the Buddhist claim is epistemic and ontological at once.
